rationales: Both findings state present-tense current timeout values (60s vs 30s) for the same field with no change/migration language explaining the difference; per constrained-mode rules this is a live disagreement, not a described update.

Both are findings asserting a present-tense timeout value for the same consumer with no change language ("changed from X to Y", "migrated") describing why or how it moved from 60s to 30s; the incoming entry just restates a new current value, so under constrained mode this is a double-current claim, not a described update.

Both entries are findings stating a current timeout value (60s vs 30s) with no change language ("changed from X to Y", "migrated") in the incoming entry—only a bare present-tense restatement—so per constrained-mode rules this is a live disagreement rather than a described update.

Both are present-tense findings about the same subject asserting incompatible propagation models (synchronous vs asynchronous with eventual consistency), with no change language ('migrated', 'changed from') to reconcile them as an update.

Both entries assert present-tense, incompatible claims about ban propagation mechanism (synchronous vs asynchronous via outbox) with no change language ("migrated to", "changed from") linking them as an update; timestamps alone don't resolve this per the priors.

Both findings state present-tense claims about the current architecture of ban propagation (synchronous vs asynchronous) with no change language ('migrated', 'changed from') linking them, so this is a live disagreement about the same fact rather than a described update.
